name: Castellote and Calanda Reservoir Loop
name_original: Sierra de Menadella – circular por el Embalse de Calanda
description: This difficult 43.0-mile touring cycling route circles the Embalse de Calanda, offering challenging terrain and scenic reservoir views.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Castellote offers diverse landscapes within the Maestrazgo region of Teruel, Aragon, Spain. The area features varied topography, from the Guadalope River valley to mountain passes like Puerto de Majalinos, which presents a challenging climb. The terrain includes dramatic cliffs, significant geological formations such as Los Órganos de Montoro, and areas within the Natura 2000 network. This provides a backdrop for routes that combine natural beauty with historical sites. Best touring cycling routes around Castellote The most popular touring cycling route is Castellote and Calanda Reservoir Loop, a 42.9 miles (69.2 km) trail that takes 5 hours 9 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain and varied terrain around the reservoir. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Villarluengo and Guadalope Gorges Loop, a difficult 21.5 miles (34.7 km) path. It traverses the scenic Guadalope Gorges, offering views of the river valley and surrounding rugged landscape. Local touring cyclists also love the Morella – Morella Castle loop from El Forcall, a 20.2 miles (32.5 km) trail leading through historical areas and towards the prominent Morella Castle, often completed in about 2 hours 25 minutes. Touring cycling around Castellote is defined by its Maestrazgo terrain, river valleys, and mountain passes. The network offers options for moderate to difficult ability levels, with 3 moderate and 20 difficult routes available. The routes in Castellote are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 6 reviews. More than 60 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Castellote's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes in Castellote?
A: Touring cycling routes around Castellote primarily cater to experienced cyclists. Out of over 20 routes, the majority (20) are classified as difficult, while 3 are moderate. This reflects the region's varied topography, including river valleys and mountain passes.

Q: Are there any challenging mountain passes for touring cyclists in the Castellote area?
A: Yes, the region features challenging climbs like the Puerto de Majalinos, a 1st category mountain pass. It spans 12.2 km with an average slope of 3.8%, offering a rewarding experience for touring cyclists seeking elevation.

Q: What natural attractions can I see while cycling around Castellote?
A: The Castellote area boasts significant natural heritage. You can encounter impressive geological formations like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2088068" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Órganos de Montoro Pass&lt;/a&gt;, explore the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5355841" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Estrecho de Crespol Gorge&lt;/a&gt;, or visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5355827" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Grutas de Cristal&lt;/a&gt;. The region also includes areas within the Natura 2000 network, offering diverse landscapes.

Q: Are there historical landmarks to explore on a touring bike in Castellote?
A: Absolutely. Castellote is rich in history, particularly its medieval past. You can cycle past the remains of the Templar &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5357652" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castellote Village and Castle&lt;/a&gt;, which offers spectacular views. Other notable historical sites include the Gothic Church of San Miguel, an aqueduct dating from 1847, and the Romanesque Shrine of the Virgen del Agua.

Q: Is there a touring cycling route that explores the Guadalope Gorges?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957100950/villarluengo-and-guadalope-gorges-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villarluengo and Guadalope Gorges Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 21.5-mile (34.7 km) path that traverses the scenic Guadalope Gorges, offering stunning views of the river valley and rugged landscape.

Q: Can I find a touring cycling route that includes a reservoir?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957149419/castellote-and-calanda-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castellote and Calanda Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 42.9-mile (69.2 km) route that features significant elevation gain and varied terrain around the reservoir.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Castellote?
A: The Maestrazgo region, where Castellote is located, experiences varied weather. Generally, spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) offer the most pleasant temperatures for touring cycling, avoiding the intense summer heat and potential winter cold.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling options in Castellote?
A: While many routes in Castellote are rated difficult, the region's diverse terrain means there might be easier sections or local paths suitable for families. It's recommended to check individual route details for distance, elevation, and surface to find the most appropriate options for your family's skill level.

Q: Are dog-friendly cycling trails available in Castellote?
A: Many natural areas and trails in the Maestrazgo region are generally accessible for dogs, especially if kept on a leash. However, specific regulations can vary, particularly within protected areas. Always check local signage and respect wildlife when cycling with your dog.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Castellote?
A: The touring cycling routes in Castellote are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the spectacular views, diverse landscapes, and the combination of natural beauty with historical sites like the Templar castle and charming villages such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5355862" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villarluengo&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5617854" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Forcall&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Can I find a touring cycling route that passes through historical towns or towards a castle?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6930228" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Morella – Morella Castle loop from El Forcall&lt;/a&gt; is a 20.2-mile (32.5 km) trail that leads through historical areas and towards the prominent Morella Castle, offering a blend of cycling and cultural exploration.

Q: Are there any touring cycling routes that are part of a larger themed trail, like 'The Silent Route'?
A: Yes, you can experience a segment of 'The Silent Route' with the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31847972" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Silent Route: The Glove loop from Ejulve&lt;/a&gt;. This difficult 54.7-mile (88 km) route offers a unique touring cycling experience through the region's quiet landscapes.
tips: This Renaissance sanctuary is carved into the rock. It was an important pilgrimage center for the neighboring towns of Teruel and Castellón, where people went to cure themselves of demonic possession (probably epilepsy).

faqs.question: What does the 'difficult' rating for this cycling route entail?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de Menadella – circular by the Embalse de Calanda route is rated 'difficult' for touring cyclists. This means it's best suited for riders with very good fitness. You should expect significant elevation changes, and potentially unpaved or rough sections where you might need to push your bike. It's a demanding route, not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: What kind of bike is recommended for this route?
faqs.answer: Given the 'difficult' rating and likely varied terrain, a bike with wider tires and robust construction is recommended. A mountain bike or a capable gravel/touring bike would be suitable, especially if you plan on carrying gear.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific percentages aren't available, the 'difficult' rating for this mountainous region suggests a varied and demanding surface. Expect a mix of terrain, potentially including unpaved or rough sections, alongside significant climbs and descents.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the Embalse de Calanda loop?
faqs.answer: You can expect picturesque natural scenery throughout the ride. The route circles the Embalse de Calanda, known for its striking turquoise blue water, set against white limestone. Pine forests are a prominent feature, especially in the Sierra de Ginebrosa, offering beautiful views.

faqs.question: Are there any notable viewpoints or landmarks on the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route features a View of the Calanda Reservoir, offering stunning vistas of the water and surrounding landscape. You will also pass by the Sanctuary of the Virgin of la Balma.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is approximately 69.2 km long with over 1000 meters of elevation gain. Komoot estimates the duration to be around 5 hours and 10 minutes of active cycling time, but this can vary significantly based on fitness level and stops.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Specific parking areas are not detailed in the available information. However, as a circular route around the Embalse de Calanda, you would typically find parking options in towns or designated areas near the reservoir, such as in Calanda itself, to begin your loop.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route would generally be during spring or autumn when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot in this region, and winters may bring colder weather or even snow at higher elevations, making the challenging terrain even more demanding.

faqs.question: Is the Sierra de Menadella – circular by the Embalse de Calanda route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed on many trails in the region, given the route's length, difficulty, and potential for varied terrain, it might be challenging for some dogs, especially if they are not accustomed to long, strenuous rides. Always ensure your dog is fit enough and bring plenty of water for them.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this cycling route or the Maestrazgo area?
faqs.answer: There are no specific permits or entrance fees mentioned for cycling this route or for general access to the Maestrazgo area where it is located. However, always respect local regulations and private property.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other official trails for varying distances. Notably, it covers sections of the Senda del Maestrazgo (8%) and the Camino del Maestrazgo-Bajo Aragón (4%). Other smaller sections include Tierra de Contrastes, Vuelta a la Atalaya y Castillo de Castellote, and Sierra de los Caballos - Santolea.
